original_bundle.answer: Yes, an easy swim is fine — soreness across both shoulders after pull-ups is muscle soreness, not a warning sign, and normal shoulder movement means the lifting itself was fine. Your data is a bit thin, so I can't tell you how deep the soreness is, but a gentle swim is the safest option: easy swimming at a comfortable pace, with a splash or pull-a-way stroke, will move the blood out of the sore muscles and back into your working heart and lungs. If the soreness spreads to your arms, chest, or neck, or if it worsens or comes with fever, run the rest of your plan by a clinician.

Yes, an easy swim is fine — soreness across both shoulders after pull-ups is muscle soreness, not a warning sign, and normal shoulder movement means the lifting itself was fine. Your data is a bit thin, so I can't tell you how deep the soreness is, but a gentle swim is the safest option: easy swimming at a comfortable pace, with a splash or pull-a-way stroke, will move the blood out of the sore muscles and back into your working heart and lungs. The number is a snapshot, so treat it as context rather than a verdict. Your resting heart rate is 56 bpm, just under your 30-day average of 57 bpm, and your recovery score is 49%, below your 67% weekly average. That combination is a normal day, not a warning. original_bundle.answer: HRV ms is your beat-to-beat heart rhythm measured in milliseconds, and higher is generally the better, so a 60 ms today is a good day to train. It's one input, not a diagnosis, so treat it as a signal, not the deciding factor. Your sleep is the dominant signal: 461 minutes, about 7.7 hours, with a 91% efficiency, and your body is absorbing it well. The one thing worth knowing: HRV doesn't measure how hard you worked, it measures how rested you were, so a high HRV doesn't mean you should skip a hard session. Today is a normal day to train, and the trend is steady: your weekly HRV has been averaging 58 ms, so a 60 ms is a normal day, not a reason to rest.
